reasoning: The Lean definition `isDifferenceSet` correctly captures the standard combinatorial notion of a difference set. The expression `List.count x [i - j]` is an indicator function (returns 1 if `x = i - j`, else 0), so the double sum counts the number of ordered pairs $(i, j)$ with $i, j \in B$, $i \neq j$, and $i - j = x$. The definition asserts the existence of a constant $k$ (the parameter $\lambda$) such that every non-zero element of $\mathbb{Z}_n$ has exactly $k$ such representations — which is exactly the classical definition of a $(v, k, \lambda)$-difference set. The restriction $j \in B \setminus \{i\}$ (i.e., $i \neq j$) matches the standard convention. For the specific claim, $|B| = 5$ gives $20$ ordered differences, matching the $20$ non-zero elements of $\mathbb{Z}_{21}$ with $\lambda = 1$. The Lean theorem is a logically equivalent formalization.
